# From linear to nonlinear mean

- So far our models are [linear]{.alert}
  - Ordinary least squares
    - Model: $Y \sim \Norm(\mu(\vx), \sigma^2)$, $\mu(\vx) = \vx^\top \vbeta$
    - Model with data: $\vY \sim \Norm(\mX\vbeta, \sigma^2 \mI)$
  - One-way ANOVA: categorical predictors, still linear mean
    - Model: $Y \sim \Norm(\mu_i, \sigma^2)$ for group $i = 1, \ldots, k$
    - Model with data: $Y_{ij} \sim \Norm(\mu_i, \sigma^2)$ independent, where $i = 1, \ldots, k$ and $j = 1, \ldots, n_i$

- What if
  - $Y \in \{0,1\}$ (success/failure) [Bernoulli]{.alert} random variable with explanatory variables $\vx$?
    - $\Ex[Y \mid \vx] = p(\vx)$ with $p(\vx) \in [0,1]$
  - But $p(\vx) = \vx^\top \vbeta$ can fall outside $[0,1]$
    - Need a [nonlinear]{.alert} function of $\vx$ to model $p(\vx)$

## Binary response setup

- Explanatory variables, $\vx \in \reals^d$ like OLS
- Response data: $Y_i \in \{0,1\}$

- Define $p_i = \Prob(Y_i = 1 \mid \vx_i)$, so $Y_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_i)$

- Need to model $p_i$ with a [nonlinear]{.alert} transformation that maps $\mathbb{R} \to (0,1)$


### Logistic function

  $$
  p_i = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\vx_i^\top \vbeta}} \qquad \text{or equivalently} \qquad \log (\text{odds}) = \logit(p_i) = \log\left(\frac{p_i}{1-p_i}\right) = \vx_i^\top \vbeta
  $$

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="60%"}

- $\beta_j = \text{change in log-odds per unit increase in } x_j$
- $e^{\beta_j} = \text{multiplicative change in odds per unit increase in } x_j$
- $\logit$ is the quantile function of the [logistic distribution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Logistic_distribution)
  - which has CDF $\displaystyle F(t) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-t}}$, $- \infty < t < \infty$


:::

::: {.column width="5%"}

&nbsp;

:::


::: {.column width="35%"}

```{python}
#| echo: false


import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.rcParams.update({

    # --- FONT FAMILY (serif + LaTeX-style math) ---
    "font.family": "serif",
    "mathtext.fontset": "stix",
    "font.serif": ["STIXGeneral"],

    # --- FONT SIZES (tuned for slides) ---
    "font.size": 18,
    "axes.titlesize": 22,
    "axes.labelsize": 20,
    "xtick.labelsize": 16,
    "ytick.labelsize": 16,
    "legend.fontsize": 22,
    "figure.titlesize": 22,

    # --- LINE / MARKER STYLE ---
    "lines.linewidth": 2.5,
    "lines.markersize": 7,

    # --- AXES STYLE ---
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.titlepad": 12,

    # --- GRID ---
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.3,

    # --- LEGEND ---
    "legend.frameon": False,
})

# x grid
x = np.linspace(-6, 6, 400)

# logistic CDF
F = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-x))

# plot
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 3))
plt.plot(x, F)
plt.xlabel("$t$")
plt.ylabel("$F(t)$")
plt.title("Logistic CDF")

plt.grid(True)
plt.show()
```

:::


:::

## Likelihood

- Bernoulli likelihood:
  $$
  L\bigl(\vbeta \mid \{(\vx_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^n\bigr)
  =
  \prod_{i=1}^n
  p_i^{y_i}(1-p_i)^{1-y_i} = \prod_{i=1}^n
  \left(\frac{1}{1 + e^{-\vx_i^\top \vbeta}}\right)^{y_i}
  \left(\frac{1}{1 + e^{\vx_i^\top \vbeta}}\right)^{1-y_i}
  $$

- Log-likelihood:
  \begin{align*}
  \ell\bigl(\vbeta \mid \{(\vx_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^n\bigr)
  & =
  \sum_{i=1}^n
  \left[
  y_i \log(p_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1-p_i) \right] \\
  & = \sum_{i=1}^n
  \left[
  y_i \log\left(\frac{1}{1 + e^{-\vx_i^\top \vbeta}}\right) + (1-y_i)\log\left(\frac{1}{1 + e^{\vx_i^\top \vbeta}}\right)
  \right]
  \end{align*}

- Estimate $\vbeta$ by MLE $\hvbeta = \argmax_\vb \ell\bigl(\vb \mid \{(\vx_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^n\bigr)$
  - No closed form solution
  - Use numerical optimization (e.g., Newton–Raphson / IRLS)

## Estimation by iteratively reweighted least squares (IRLS)

- MLE: $\hvbeta = \arg\max_{\vb} \ell(\vb)$

- Solve: $\nabla \ell(\vbeta)=0$ (no closed form)

- Iteratively reweighted least squares (IRLS):

  - Initialize $\vbeta^{(0)} = \vzero$

  - For $t = 0,1,2,\ldots$ until convergence:

    - $\vp^{(t)} = \bigl(p_1^{(t)},\dots,p_n^{(t)}\bigr)^\top$,  
      $p_i^{(t)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-\vx_i^\top \vbeta^{(t)}}}$ predicted probabilities of success for data

    - $\mW^{(t)} = \diag\!\bigl(p_i^{(t)}(1-p_i^{(t)})\bigr)$ diagaonal matrix of regression weights

    - $\vz^{(t)} = \mX\vbeta^{(t)} + \bigl(\mW^{(t)}\bigr)^{-1}(\vy-\vp^{(t)})$, where $\vy-\vp^{(t)}$ is the vector of residuals

    - Update:
      $$
      \vbeta^{(t+1)}
      =
    \arg\min_{\vb}
    \sum_{i=1}^n
    w_i^{(t)}\bigl(z_i^{(t)} - \vx_i^\top \vb\bigr)^2
      =
      \left(\mX^\top \mW^{(t)} \mX\right)^{-1}
      \mX^\top \mW^{(t)} \vz^{(t)}
      $$


## Inference

- MLE: $\hvbeta = \arg\max_{\vb} \ell(\vb)$

- Approximate distribution: $\hvbeta \appxsim \Norm\!\bigl(\vbeta,\; (\mX^\top \mW \mX)^{-1}\bigr)$

- Weights: $\mW = \diag\bigl(p_i(1-p_i)\bigr)$

- Standard errors: $\SE(\hbeta_j) = \sqrt{\bigl[(\mX^\top \mW \mX)^{-1}\bigr]_{jj}}$

- Wald statistic: $\displaystyle \frac{\hat\beta_j}{\text{SE}(\hat\beta_j)} \approx \Norm(0,1)$

- Likelihood ratio statistic for nested models: $2\bigl(\ell(\text{full})-\ell(\text{reduced})\bigr) \approx \chi^2_\nu$

- Same philosophy as OLS: test whether predictors matter

## Decision boundary (prediction)

- For a new $\vx$: $\hp(\vx) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-\vx^\top \hvbeta}}$

- Classification: $\hY(\vx) = 1$ if $\hp(\vx) > 0.5$, else $0$

- Boundary: $\hp(\vx)=0.5 \iff \vx^\top \hvbeta = 0$

- Uncertainty in $\hp(\vx)$ comes from $\hvbeta$:
  $$
  \text{SE}\bigl(\hp(\vx)\bigr)
  \approx
  \hp(\vx)\bigl(1-\hp(\vx)\bigr)
  \sqrt{\vx^\top(\mX^\top \mW \mX)^{-1}\vx}
  $$

- Confidence interval for probability:
  $\hp(\vx) \pm z_{\alpha/2}\,\text{SE}\bigl(\hp(\vx)\bigr)$

- Classification confidence:
  - uncertain if $\hp(\vx) \approx 0.5$
  - more confident as $\hp(\vx)$ moves away from $0.5$


## Probit link

- Logit: $\logit(p) = \displaystyle \log\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)$ is the [quantile function]{.alert} of the logistic distribution

- Probit: $\probit(p) = \Phi^{-1}(p)$ is the [quantile function]{.alert} of the standard normal distribution
  - Model is $\probit(p(\vx)) = \vx^\top \vbeta$
  - $p(\vx) = \Phi(\vx^\top \vbeta)$, where $\Phi$ is the CDF of $\Norm(0,1)$


::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="35%"}

- Both:
  - map $(0,1) \to \reals$
  - give similar fits

- probit interpretation:
  - latent normal threshold model
  - lighter tails than logit

:::

::: {.column width="5%"}
&nbsp;
:::

::: {.column width="60%"}
```{python}
#| echo: false

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.stats import norm

# --- assume your rcParams already set above ---

# grid
x = np.linspace(-6, 6, 400)

# CDFs
F_logistic = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-x))
F_normal = norm.cdf(x * np.sqrt(3) / np.pi)

# figure
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))

# plots
ax.plot(x, F_logistic, label="Logistic (logit)")
ax.plot(x, F_normal, linestyle="--", label="Normal (probit)")

# labels
ax.set_xlabel("$t$")
ax.set_ylabel("$F(t)$")
ax.set_title(r"Logistic vs Normal CDF (variance $\mathbf{matched}$)")

# clean bounds
ax.set_ylim(-0.05, 1.05)

# legend
ax.legend()

plt.show()
```

:::

:::

## Example: Graduate admissions explained by GRE

Suppose that we observe whether an applicant is admitted to a graduate program

$$
Y =
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if admitted} \\
0, & \text{if not admitted}
\end{cases}
\qquad \text{and} \qquad
X = \text{GRE score}
$$

Does the probability of admission depend on the applicant's GRE score?

$$
p(x) = \Prob(Y=1 \mid X=x)
$$

A logistic regression model assumes that

$$
\log\left(\frac{p(x)}{1-p(x)}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x \qquad \text{or equivalently} \qquad
p(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0+\beta_1 x)}}
$$

Thus, the probability of admission is an S-shaped function of the GRE score

---

### Simulated data with realistic GRE scores

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import statsmodels.api as sm

np.random.seed(20260317)

n = 120

# Realistic GRE scores: not evenly spaced
gre = np.random.normal(loc=305, scale=11, size=n)
gre = np.clip(gre, 260, 340)
gre = np.round(gre).astype(int)

# True model used to simulate the data
beta0 = -24.0
beta1 = 0.08

eta = beta0 + beta1 * gre
p_admit = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-eta))
admit = np.random.binomial(1, p_admit)

df1 = pd.DataFrame({
    "GRE": gre,
    "p_admit_true": p_admit,
    "admit": admit
})

df1.sample(12, random_state=20260317).sort_values("GRE")
```

---

### Plot of the binary data and the true logistic curve

```{python}
gre_plot = np.linspace(260, 340, 300)
p_plot = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(beta0 + beta1 * gre_plot)))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 7))

ax.scatter(df1["GRE"], df1["admit"], alpha=0.45)
ax.plot(gre_plot, p_plot, linewidth=2)

ax.set_xlabel("GRE score")
ax.set_ylabel("Admission outcome / probability")
ax.set_title("Graduate admissions: GRE-only true logistic model")
plt.show()
```

---

### Fit a logistic regression model using GRE only

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="35%"}

```{python}
X1 = sm.add_constant(df1[["GRE"]])
fit1 = sm.Logit(df1["admit"], X1).fit(disp=False)
params1 = fit1.params
se1 = fit1.bse

table1 = pd.DataFrame({
    "Coefficient": params1,
    "Std. Error": se1
})

table1.round(4)
```

:::

::: {.column width="5%"}
&nbsp;
::: 

::: {.column width="60%"}

```{python}
p_hat1_plot = fit1.predict(sm.add_constant(pd.DataFrame({"GRE": gre_plot}), has_constant="add"))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 7))

ax.scatter(df1["GRE"], df1["admit"], alpha=0.35, label="Observed data")
ax.plot(gre_plot, p_plot, linewidth=2, linestyle="-", label="True probability")
ax.plot(gre_plot, p_hat1_plot, linewidth=2, linestyle="--", label="Fitted probability")

ax.set_xlabel("GRE score")
ax.set_ylabel("Admission outcome / probability")
ax.set_title("GRE-only model: true and fitted logistic curves")
ax.legend()
plt.show()
```

:::

:::


---

## Example: Graduate admissions explained by both GRE and GPA

Admissions are not based on GRE alone.  A more realistic model allows the admission probability to depend on both GRE and GPA:

$$
\log\left(\frac{p(x_1,x_2)}{1-p(x_1,x_2)}\right)
=
\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2,
$$

where

- $x_1$ is GRE

- $x_2$ is GPA

We cannot visualize this model with a single probability curve against one predictor, but we can still explore model fit through fitted probabilities and grouped summaries

---

### Simulated data with GRE and GPA

```{python}
np.random.seed(563)

n = 160

gre = np.random.normal(loc=304, scale=11, size=n)
gre = np.clip(gre, 260, 340)
gre = np.round(gre).astype(int)

gpa = np.random.normal(loc=3.35, scale=0.28, size=n)
gpa = np.clip(gpa, 2.4, 4.0)
gpa = np.round(gpa, 2)

# True model used to simulate the data
beta0 = -27.5
beta1 = 0.065
beta2 = 2.3

eta = beta0 + beta1 * gre + beta2 * gpa
p_admit = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-eta))
admit = np.random.binomial(1, p_admit)

df2 = pd.DataFrame({
    "GRE": gre,
    "GPA": gpa,
    "p_admit_true": p_admit,
    "admit": admit
})

df2.sample(12, random_state=563).sort_values(["GRE", "GPA"])
```

---



:::{.columns}
::: {.column width="45%"}

### Fit a logistic regression model using GRE and GPA
```{python}
X2 = sm.add_constant(df2[["GRE", "GPA"]])
fit2 = sm.Logit(df2["admit"], X2).fit(disp=False)
params2 = fit2.params
se2 = fit2.bse
table2 = pd.DataFrame({
    "Coefficient": params2,
    "Std. Error": se2
})
table2.round(4)
```
:::

::: {.column width="5%"}
&nbsp;
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

### Predicted probabilities for a few applicants

```{python}
df2_show = df2.copy()
df2_show["p_admit_hat"] = fit2.predict(X2)

df2_show.loc[:, ["GRE", "GPA", "admit", "p_admit_hat"]].sample(12, random_state=2026) \
    .sort_values(["GRE", "GPA"]) \
    .round({"p_admit_hat": 3})
```

:::

:::

---

### Compare observed and fitted admission rates by decile of fitted probability

```{python}
df2_check = df2.copy()
df2_check["p_admit_hat"] = fit2.predict(X2)

df2_check["prob_bin"] = pd.qcut(df2_check["p_admit_hat"], q=10, duplicates="drop")

calibration_table = (
    df2_check
    .groupby("prob_bin", observed=False)
    .agg(
        n=("admit", "size"),
        mean_fitted_probability=("p_admit_hat", "mean"),
        observed_admission_rate=("admit", "mean")
    )
    .reset_index()
)

calibration_table.round({
    "mean_fitted_probability": 3,
    "observed_admission_rate": 3
})
```

---

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}

### Calibration plot

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))

ax.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], linestyle="--")
ax.scatter(
    calibration_table["mean_fitted_probability"],
    calibration_table["observed_admission_rate"]
)

ax.set_xlabel("Mean fitted probability")
ax.set_ylabel("Observed admission rate")
ax.set_title("GRE + GPA model: calibration by probability groups")
plt.show()
```

::: 

::: {.column width="5%"}
&nbsp;
:::

::: {.column width="55%"}

### Interpretation

- GRE-only model: probability of admission is a curve as a function of one predictor
- GRE + GPA model: probability depends on two predictors, so  no single S-shaped curve to plot against one horizontal axis
- Can still assess fit by examining fitted probabilities and comparing them with observed admission rates in groups

This is why regression is often explored using:

- fitted probabilities
- grouped summaries
- calibration plots
- classification tables

rather than only a single scatterplot with a fitted curve

:::

:::


## Big picture

- Linear model models mean directly

- Logistic regression models transformed mean (log-odds)

- Key idea: keep linear structure, change the scale


## From logit to a general framework

- Logistic regression:
  - $Y \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p)$
  - $\logit(p) = \vx^\top \vbeta$

- Key idea: linear model on a transformed mean

### Generalized linear models

- Form: $g(\mu) = \vx^\top \vbeta$, where $\mu = \Ex[Y]$

- Three components:
  - distribution of $Y$
  - linear predictor $\vx^\top \vbeta$
  - link function $g$

Linear structure stays, only the transformation, $g$, changes

## Examples

- OLS: $Y_i \sim \Norm(\mu_i,\sigma^2)$, $g(\mu)=\mu$

- Logistic: $Y_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_i)$, $g(p)=\log\frac{p}{1-p}$

- Poisson: $Y_i \sim \text{Poisson}(\mu_i)$, $g(\mu)=\log \mu$

### Links

- Match the range:
  - probabilities in $(0,1)$
  - counts $\ge 0$

- Make model linear:
  - $g(\mu_i)$ linear in $\vx_i$


## Variance is built in

- OLS: $\var(Y_i)=\sigma^2$

- Logistic: $\var(Y_i)=p_i(1-p_i)$

- Poisson: $\var(Y_i)=\mu_i$

- Mean determines variance


## Estimation and inference

- Likelihood: $L(\vbeta) = \prod f(y_i \mid \mu_i)$

- Solve: $\nabla \ell(\vbeta)=0$ (no closed form)

- Computation: IRLS (generalizes least squares)

- Inference:
  - Wald tests  
  - likelihood ratio tests

## Big picture

- Linear model:
  - models mean directly

- GLM:
  - models transformed mean

- Choose:
  - distribution  
  - link  

- Covers:
  - regression, ANOVA, classification, counts
